video_title: Michael Howell: AI Will Fuel Inflation, And Markets Aren’t Ready
gist: Michael Howell argues the global liquidity cycle has peaked and is rolling over—not because central banks are tightening, but because a booming US real economy (AI capex, Treasury QE) is sucking money out of financial markets. He reads classic late-cycle signals (bear-flattening curve, strong commodities, hot inflation), expects the AI capex boom to be inflationary near-term, sees China—not the Fed—as the marginal driver of gold, and thinks markets are mispricing both inflation and the risk of Fed hikes.

themes.theme: Global liquidity cycle peaking & rolling over

insights.claim: Michael Howell's global liquidity models show the worldwide liquidity cycle has peaked and the underlying momentum (black line) is inflecting lower, even though absolute liquidity is still 'inching' higher—signaling the easy-money phase is over.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: A peaking/rolling-over liquidity impulse historically precedes PE compression and rising volatility—reduce risk gradually rather than waiting for an absolute liquidity decline.
insights.quote: we're around the top of the cycle in terms of liquidity impulses

insights.claim: US financial liquidity is dropping because money is shifting OUT of financial markets and INTO the real economy (manufacturing/services), so weak financial-liquidity prints reflect economic strength, not a credit crunch—'all money that is anywhere must be somewhere.'
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Falling financial liquidity amid a strong economy is bearish for asset multiples even as earnings stay strong—watch the financial vs. real-economy split, not headline growth.
insights.quote: there's a giant sucking sound as money is being drawn out of financial markets

insights.claim: AI capex is itself a mechanism draining the financial system: big tech is funding datacenter spend by pulling cash out of corporate treasury departments that previously sat in financial markets, directly reducing financial liquidity.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: The AI boom is liquidity-negative for financial assets at the margin—the more capex accelerates, the more it tightens the plumbing under valuations.
insights.quote: where have they been getting the money from? They've been taking it out of their treasury departments

insights.claim: Howell maps markets through four sequential regime quadrants—calm, speculation, turbulence, rebound—and places the market firmly in 'speculation' now, with 'turbulence' (where investors typically lose money) as the next phase.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Speculation-phase returns are real but low-quality (high vol); position defensively ahead of the turbulence transition rather than chasing late-cycle gains.
insights.quote: we're in speculation now. There's no question about that... We've left calm behind

insights.claim: The Federal Reserve has injected roughly $600 billion back into money markets since late October to offset funding strains and maintain financial-market stability against the real-economy liquidity drain.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: The Fed is already easing financial-plumbing stress quietly—its 'generosity' is masking underlying fragility, so don't read calm markets as a sign the system is healthy.
insights.quote: the Fed itself has put about 600 billion effectively back into money markets

insights.claim: The US Treasury is actively suppressing bond-market volatility via Treasury buybacks—distinct from formal yield-curve control but a clear intervention to hold the long end down.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Suppressed long-end yields are policy-engineered, not market-cleared—the retreat in 30-year yields is artificial and could reverse hard if intervention fails.
insights.quote: the Treasury which is actively trying to suppress bond volatility by undertaking treasury buybacks

insights.claim: Howell frames the core policy dilemma as self-reinforcing: a worse fiscal picture pressures the bond market, and more bond-market pressure raises financial-stability risk—trapping the Fed/Treasury between funding the deficit and preventing a rupture.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Authorities are 'handcuffed by the market they have'—the more they intervene, the more they signal cracks; tail risk of a bond-market accident is rising.
insights.quote: are you worried maybe a bit more like us that the system is starting to see cracks

insights.claim: Howell argues the AI capex boom is inflationary in the short term regardless of AI's long-run merits, and the Fed must react to that inflation now rather than pre-emptively assuming long-run deflation.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Positions betting on AI-driven disinflation are premature—near-term the capex surge adds to price pressure and argues against rate cuts.
insights.quote: whatever the merits of AI, in the short term, it's inflationary for the economy

insights.claim: Howell directly disputes Fed nominee Kevin Warsh's view that AI's deflationary impact justifies forward guidance, calling fixed deflationary anticipation 'the wrong thing to have' given no current evidence of AI deflation.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: A Warsh-led Fed leaning dovish on AI-deflation grounds could fall behind the inflation curve—watch for policy error risk.
insights.quote: he's already got a view a fixed view about what this is going to do and I think that's the wrong thing to have

insights.claim: Technology is deflationary for the product itself but not the broad economy: Howell expects AI/compute prices to tumble due to massive investment, citing Global Crossing—where fiber-optic cable prices fell ~90% and the company went bust within ~5 years of its Salomon IPO.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Expect AI output/compute prices to collapse over time and capex-heavy AI names to face the Global Crossing fate—deflation hits the product, raising obsolescence risk for the investment.
insights.quote: the price of fiber optic cable... fell by 90%

insights.claim: With US nominal GDP growth plausibly 7-8% and long-term Treasury yields historically tracking nominal GDP, Howell sees structural upward pressure pushing the US long bond well above its current ~5% level.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: The fair-value gap implies materially higher long yields—bearish for duration and a direct threat to deficit funding and equity multiples.
insights.quote: typically over the long term the fixed income markets the yield on the long-term treasury tends to match N GDP growth

insights.claim: Markets are seeing a bear-FLATTENING of the yield curve (short-end rising faster than long-end), the opposite of the consensus bear-steepening call from January 1—a classic late-cycle signal nobody positioned for.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: A bear-flattener confirms late-cycle dynamics and wrong-foots steepener positioning—trade the flattening and treat it as a turbulence warning.
insights.quote: The consensus view among fixed income investors on January 1 this year was you would get basically a bare steepening... You had a bare flattening

insights.claim: Howell says it is too early to buy the long end of the bond market, but mid-duration (out to ~5-year) bonds offer enough carry to protect downside even if yields rise across the curve over the next 12 months.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Stay short-to-mid duration: cash through ~5-year bonds gives carry-protected downside; avoid the long end until yields top out.
insights.quote: it's too early to buy the long end of the bond market yet

themes.theme: Earnings vs. multiples (E vs PE) framework

insights.claim: Howell separates equity returns into the E (earnings, the trend) and the PE (the cycle), arguing the PE often dominates and is set to derate as liquidity shifts to the real economy and inflation/yields rise—even as earnings stay strong.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Strong earnings won't save equities if the PE compresses—the market 'should be derating' in anticipation of higher inflation and yields.
insights.quote: the E may be the trend but the PE is really the cycle. And often PE dominates the E

insights.claim: Howell notes stocks and bonds only hedge each other in a low, stable-inflation window; in higher inflation BOTH do badly, and only in deflation do bonds outperform—implying the classic 60/40 diversification breaks down in the inflationary regime he expects.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: In a rising-inflation regime, stock-bond correlation turns positive and diversification fails—look to commodities/gold rather than bonds for portfolio protection.
insights.quote: once you start to get into a higher inflation environment bonds do badly, stocks do badly

themes.theme: China as the marginal driver of gold

insights.claim: Howell argues China—not Western debasement-trade buyers, the Fed or the ECB—is the marginal driver of the gold price, with the Shanghai Gold Exchange now setting the marginal price of world bullion.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Track PBOC liquidity and Chinese demand—not the Fed or Western ETF flows—to anticipate gold's direction.
insights.quote: the big driver of gold has not been what has been happening from the Fed or from the ECB... It's basically been China

insights.claim: Gold's recent weakness is explained by the PBOC abruptly slashing net liquidity injections—Howell shows PBOC liquidity 'fell off a cliff' coinciding with the gold pullback, with that move possibly tied to clamping Chinese retail speculation.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: The gold dip is a China-liquidity event, not a structural top—watch PBOC injection data for the restart signal to re-enter gold.
insights.quote: if the Chinese authorities hit the brakes uh on liquidity injections, that will uh undermine the gold market

insights.claim: Chinese gold demand is structurally driven by capital controls: residents can't buy crypto and channel monetary-inflation hedging into the only permitted stores of value—Chinese stocks, real estate, or gold—with gold the favored choice.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Structural retail gold demand persists as long as capital controls and domestic monetary inflation do—a durable bid under bullion.
insights.quote: Chinese residents are not allowed to buy crypto... what they can buy are Chinese stocks, real estate uh or gold

insights.claim: Howell expects China's gold buying to continue because Beijing is using gold as the collateral base to internationalize the yuan (lacking a recognized international bond market)—not a gold standard, but a security backing to compete with the dollar.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Sovereign Chinese gold accumulation is strategic and price-insensitive—expect a structural long-term uptrend in gold underpinned at $4,000-$5,000/oz.
insights.quote: they are using gold as the collateral base for the yuan

insights.claim: Howell believes China must devalue the yuan internally (print money, devalue currency) to escape its debt overhang—history's standard debt exit—which would raise the gold price in yuan terms significantly while capital controls shield the offshore yuan.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Domestic Chinese money-printing to inflate away debt is a tailwind for yuan-denominated gold—bullish gold, with limited offshore-yuan spillover.
insights.quote: if they print money domestically, they will devalue debt and they will basically raise the gold price in nuance significantly

insights.claim: Howell estimates China's true gold reserves are far above its declared ~2,000 tons—'more likely six [thousand]'—versus ~8,000 tons at US Fort Knox, with the accumulation deliberately hidden.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Understated official reserves imply ongoing covert accumulation—a persistent, underreported sovereign bid for gold.
insights.quote: The numbers that they uh they claim to have uh are probably about 2,000 tons... more likely six, but they've effectively, you know, they've they've hidden that

themes.theme: Oil, gold/oil ratio & the $200 oil case

insights.claim: Using the gold/oil ratio (long-run mean ~20x an ounce of gold per barrel, stable from 1970 to 2022 across vastly different price levels) and gold underpinned near $4,000-$5,000, Howell triangulates an implied oil price above $200/barrel.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: If the 20x ratio holds and China underpins gold, oil is structurally cheap—a contrarian case for triple-digit-plus oil over a 2-3 year mean-reversion window.
insights.quote: Divide that by 20. What have you got? Over $200 a barrel of oil

insights.claim: Howell flags the $200 oil view as so far from consensus ('absolutely nobody thought I was sane') that, as a contrarian, it's worth contemplating—while stressing he's clearer on direction (up) than level.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Extreme positioning against higher oil creates asymmetric upside if global growth/China traction erodes the perceived oil surplus.
insights.quote: absolutely nobody thought I was sane uh saying that this gold the oil could go to 200

insights.claim: Howell argues the widely-cited oil 'surplus' could vanish quickly if the world economy and China gain traction, tightening the market—and notes inventories have already been drawn down, with supply-chain logistics (not just supply) influencing price.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: A demand pickup plus drawn-down inventories could flip the oil narrative from glut to tightness—adding to the inflation impulse.

insights.claim: Howell treats crypto as a leading barometer of global liquidity: he overlays a 60/30/10 Bitcoin/Ethereum/Solana basket against liquidity advanced by 13 weeks (~3 months), and the relationship is 'approximately right.'
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Use crypto price action as a real-time read on the liquidity cycle, and use liquidity (led ~13 weeks) to anticipate crypto—both currently point lower.
insights.quote: crypto is a fantastic barometer of underlying liquidity conditions

insights.claim: Howell attributes current Bitcoin weakness and large Bitcoin-ETF outflows to the rolling-over liquidity cycle rather than anything fundamentally broken in crypto—a liquidity-driven down-leg, not a structural break.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Crypto weakness is further confirmation the liquidity cycle is rolling over—expect continued pressure while liquidity ebbs, but it's cyclical not terminal.

insights.claim: Howell expects the Fed will have to RAISE rates within the next 12 months because of an inflation problem—a sharply non-consensus call against a market positioned for cuts.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: A Fed hike within 12 months would blindside rate-cut positioning—hedge against higher short rates and a hawkish surprise.
insights.quote: I think the Fed is going to have to raise rates in the next 12 months

insights.claim: Howell says the TIPS market—pricing ~2.6% 10-year breakeven inflation—is not accurately pricing inflation risk, given the Fed has missed its 2% target for ~62 months; he calls the 2% target 'fantasy.'
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Underpriced breakevens make TIPS / inflation protection cheap relative to Howell's regime—an asymmetric inflation hedge.
insights.quote: implied inflation, 10 year inflation 2.6 or there or thereabouts... I mean, the 2% is fantasy

insights.claim: Howell draws a strong 1970s analog: a series of seemingly unconnected supply shocks (oil, then coffee, sugar) that markets failed to 'join the dots' on until forced to—and sees today's commodity moves (Strait of Hormuz, fertilizers, helium) the same way, with soft commodities 'primed to move a lot higher.'
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Treat scattered commodity supply shocks as a connected inflation regime—overweight commodities, especially soft commodities, as a 1970s-style inflation hedge.
insights.quote: soft commodities are you know primed to to move a lot higher here

insights.claim: Howell's bottom-line advice to a Warsh-led Fed is to start tightening quickly in anticipation of a bigger inflation problem—while predicting it won't happen because pre-emptive tightening is politically very difficult, which is precisely why inflation problems recur.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Expect the Fed to be politically constrained from pre-emptive tightening—base case is a behind-the-curve Fed and a more entrenched inflation problem.
insights.quote: they've got to start tightening pretty quickly in anticipation of a bigger inflation problem. That won't happen, I don't think

insights.claim: Howell frames US-China as a 'capital wars' clash for monetary supremacy, with China building gold-backed collateral to internationalize the yuan precisely because it lacks a recognized international bond market to anchor it.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: The dollar-yuan collateral contest is a multi-year structural driver of gold demand and reserve-asset flows.
insights.quote: my my world is a world of capital wars... the clash of capitals between America and China

insights.claim: Despite long-term threats, Howell remains firmly in the dollar-dominance camp: surplus nations have no viable alternative (Swiss franc too small, euro legally in the dollar camp and facing its own questions), so they must hold dollar assets.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: Don't bet on near-term dollar dethronement—the dollar stays 'king' for transactions even as its level is debatable; reserve diversification flows to gold, not rival currencies.
insights.quote: in terms of transactions the dollar is is still king

insights.claim: Howell flags a structural vulnerability: as the marginal buyer of Treasuries shifts from foreign sovereigns to hedge funds, the US collateral base (linked to oil via OPEC pricing/recycling into Treasuries) becomes more fragile.
insights.speaker: Michael Howell
insights.so_what: A hedge-fund-dominated Treasury bid is less sticky than sovereign demand—raising the risk of bond-market dislocations under stress.
